skill.name: incident-runbook-templates
skill.description: Create structured incident response runbooks with step-by-step procedures, escalation paths, and recovery actions. Use when building runbooks, responding to incidents, or establishing incident response procedures.
skill.summary: Create structured incident response runbooks for outages, database incidents, escalation, and recovery.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, URL, IP address, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns in SKILL.md. Review found these are Markdown runbook examples and false positives, not executable skill code or malicious behavior. The skill is safe to publish with a low-risk warning because operators may copy impactful production commands.

low_findings.title: Operational Command Examples Require Review
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE as content risk, not executable malware. SKILL.md contains Kubernetes, curl, psql, and shell command examples for incident response. They are inside Markdown templates, so the skill does not execute them, but users could cause production changes if they copy commands without adapting them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clearly present in Markdown code blocks for runbook examples. There is no script wrapper or automatic execution path in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Example URLs and IP Ranges Are Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for network exfiltration. The hardcoded URLs point to example dashboards, status pages, documentation, local Prometheus, and placeholder company APIs. The IP ranges appear in an example Kubernetes NetworkPolicy, not in active network code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs and IP ranges are embedded in documentation examples and resource links. No code sends secrets or collected data to these endpoints.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-cryptography static findings map to ordinary words or SQL ordering text in Markdown content, not cryptographic algorithm use. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA1, or custom cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain frontmatter text or SQL ORDER BY clauses. They do not define or invoke cryptographic primitives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Copy-Paste Production Change Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes rollback, scaling, feature-flag, backend termination, and vacuum examples. These are valid runbook patterns, but marketplace users should treat them as templates that require service-specific review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples include commands that can alter live infrastructure or databases. The risk is operational misuse, not automatic execution by the skill.

content.value_statement: Incident teams need clear response steps before an outage starts. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code draft structured runbooks with triage, mitigation, escalation, and communication sections.
actual_capabilities: Creates service outage runbooks with impact assessment, detection, triage, mitigation, rollback, and verification sections.
Provides database incident templates for connection pools, replication lag, locks, disk space, and recovery steps.
Drafts escalation matrices that map severity, duration, business impact, and owner contacts.
Generates internal incident notification, status update, and resolution message templates.
Suggests runbook best practices for testing, postmortems, rollback planning, and dashboard links.
limitations: Does not validate commands against a live production environment.
Uses placeholder service names, dashboards, APIs, and contacts that must be customized.
Cannot replace incident commander judgment during active response.
Does not automatically execute, monitor, or remediate incidents.

use_cases.title: Prepare Service Outage Runbooks
use_cases.description: Create a standard response document for a critical application before it fails in production.

use_cases.title: Standardize Database Incident Response
use_cases.description: Document checks and recovery actions for connection spikes, replication lag, locks, and disk pressure.

use_cases.title: Onboard New On-Call Engineers
use_cases.description: Give responders a consistent template for severity levels, escalation, communication, rollback, and verification.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incident runbook for [service name]. Include overview, impact, alerts, initial triage, mitigation, rollback, verification, escalation, and communication sections.

prompt_templates.title: Customize for a Production Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Adapt the service outage runbook for [service]. Use these details: owner [team], namespace [namespace], alerts [alerts], dashboards [links], dependencies [list], and rollback method [method].

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Database Incident Procedure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a database incident runbook for [database system]. Cover connection pool exhaustion, slow queries, replication lag, locks, disk space, failover criteria, and post-incident verification.

prompt_templates.title: Review and Harden an Existing Runbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this runbook for missing safety checks, unclear escalation criteria, risky commands, rollback gaps, and weak verification steps. Return a revised version with safer operator guidance.

output_examples.input: Create a payment service outage runbook.
output_examples.output: A structured runbook with payment service ownership, impact questions, alert checks, Kubernetes triage steps, rollback guidance, dependency checks, verification tasks, escalation rules, and stakeholder messages.

output_examples.input: Make a database incident runbook for replication lag.
output_examples.output: A database response document covering lag detection, primary and replica health checks, disk and network checks, failover decision criteria, customer impact notes, and recovery verification.

output_examples.input: Improve this runbook for on-call use.
output_examples.output: A revised runbook with clearer severity labels, safer command notes, explicit stop conditions, named escalation owners, communication templates, and postmortem follow-up items.

best_practices: Replace all placeholder commands, URLs, namespaces, and contacts before using a runbook in production.
Test each runbook during drills and update it after every real incident.
Include rollback, verification, escalation, and communication steps for every mitigation path.
anti_patterns: Do not copy production-changing commands without peer review and environment-specific validation.
Do not rely on generic severity labels without defining customer and business impact.
Do not omit owner contacts, escalation deadlines, or post-incident follow-up actions.

faq.question: Does this skill execute incident response commands?
faq.answer: No. It provides Markdown templates and example commands. Operators must review and run commands manually.

faq.question: Can I use it during an active incident?
faq.answer: Yes, but treat the output as guidance. Incident commanders should verify each step against the live environment.

faq.question: Which incident types are covered?
faq.answer: The templates focus on service outages, high latency, partial failures, traffic surges, and database incidents.

faq.question: Does it include communication templates?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for initial notification, status updates, and resolution messages.

faq.question: What should I customize first?
faq.answer: Update service names, owners, alerts, dashboards, namespaces, dependencies, rollback steps, and escalation contacts.

faq.question: Is it suitable for regulated environments?
faq.answer: It can provide a starting point, but legal, compliance, and security teams should review final runbooks.
